title: Data Flow
intro: Data flows through your task manager like water through pipes — from the database, through the API, into the UI, and back again. Just like tracing a package through its delivery route, understanding this path helps you find where things go wrong. Swipe right on correct data flow patterns, left on incorrect ones.

teach.title: The Round Trip of a Task
teach.body: When a user creates a task, the data travels a specific path: **UI form -> API call -> Supabase client -> PostgreSQL database**. When they load the task list, it reverses: **Database -> Supabase API -> your app -> rendered in the UI**. Understanding this round trip helps you debug problems — if tasks aren't showing up, you can check each step in the chain to find where it breaks.

teach.title: The Restaurant Analogy
teach.body: Think of data flow like ordering at a restaurant. The user (customer) fills out a form (places an order). The frontend (waiter) sends it to the API (kitchen). The database (pantry) stores the ingredients. When you fetch data, the kitchen prepares the dish from the pantry and the waiter brings it to the table. If the food is wrong, you trace the problem: did the waiter write down the wrong order, did the kitchen misread it, or was the pantry stocked wrong?

teach.title: Data Flow Is Like a Package Delivery
teach.body: When you order something online, the package goes: your order -> warehouse -> delivery truck -> your door. If it never arrives, you can track each step: did the warehouse ship it? Is the truck stuck? Was the address wrong? Data in your app follows the same traceable path. When something doesn't show up in the UI, you check each stop along the route.

Understanding data flow is the superpower that lets you debug anything. When you can trace a bug from button click to database and back, you build reliable apps — the kind people trust with their daily workflow and gladly pay for.

Data flows in a predictable path: UI -> API -> Database and back. When something breaks, trace the flow step by step. Check the browser's network tab for API errors, Supabase logs for database errors, and the UI for rendering issues.

cards.content: Updating the UI immediately when a user completes a task, then syncing with the database in the background
cards.explanation: This is called an optimistic update. It makes the app feel instant. If the database update fails, you revert the UI and show an error. Most modern apps use this pattern.

cards.content: Having the browser connect directly to PostgreSQL using a database connection string
cards.explanation: Never expose database credentials to the browser. The Supabase client uses a secure API layer with authentication — it never gives the browser direct database access.

cards.content: Showing a loading spinner while fetching tasks from the database, then rendering the list when the data arrives
cards.explanation: Database calls take time (network latency). Loading states prevent the UI from looking broken or empty during the fetch. Always handle the loading, success, and error states.

cards.content: Assuming every database call succeeds and not handling errors in your data fetching code
cards.explanation: Network requests fail. Databases go down. Users lose internet. Always wrap data calls in try/catch and show meaningful error messages instead of a blank screen or cryptic crash.

cards.content: Using Supabase Row Level Security so the database filters data by user, rather than filtering only in your app code
cards.explanation: RLS is defense in depth. Even if your frontend has a bug that forgets a user filter, the database itself enforces the access rules. This is a critical security pattern.

cards.content: Fetching the entire task list from the database every second to check for changes
cards.explanation: Polling every second wastes bandwidth and database resources. Use Supabase's realtime subscriptions to get notified of changes, or re-fetch only when the user takes an action.

cards.content: Validating user input on both the frontend and the backend before inserting data into the database
cards.explanation: Frontend validation gives instant feedback. Backend validation prevents malicious or malformed data from ever reaching the database. You need both — frontend alone can be bypassed.
